question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a nil-pointer dereference panic by supplying a dbConfig that causes gorm.Open() to fail, since the code calls log.Warn(err) and continues to use the nil db object on line 25 (db.DB()), leading to a server crash (DoS)?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user cause ConnectToDb() to return nil (line 29) by providing a dbConfig where gorm.Open() succeeds but db.DB() fails, and if callers do not check for nil, does this propagate a nil-dereference panic through the application?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that a failed gorm.Open() only logs a warning (log.Warn(err)) rather than halting execution, allowing the application to silently continue in a broken state where all subsequent DB operations fail unpredictably?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user cause information disclosure by triggering a DB connection error, since log.Warn(err) and log.Errorf() may emit internal DSN details (host, port, username, dbname) into logs that are accessible via a log-streaming endpoint?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the hardcoded sslmode=disable in GetDsn() (config/types.go line 51) to perform a man-in-the-middle attack on the DB connection, intercepting plaintext credentials and query results in transit?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user who can observe network traffic between the Rosetta service and the database read plaintext SQL queries and responses because sslmode=disable forces an unencrypted connection established by ConnectToDb()?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of TLS (sslmode=disable in GetDsn()) to inject forged SQL responses into the unencrypted DB connection, causing the Rosetta API to return attacker-controlled data?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a connection pool exhaustion DoS by sending a high volume of API requests that each consume a DB connection, given that MaxOpenConnections is configurable and may be set to an unbounded or very large value via dbConfig.Pool.MaxOpenConnections?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user exhaust all idle DB connections by triggering many concurrent requests, since sqlDb.SetMaxIdleConns(dbConfig.Pool.MaxIdleConnections) is set at startup and there is no per-user or per-IP connection limit enforced at the DB layer?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user cause resource starvation by exploiting the absence of a minimum value check on dbConfig.Pool.MaxOpenConnections, allowing it to be set to 0 (unlimited) and enabling unbounded connection creation that exhausts the PostgreSQL server's connection limit?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a slow-connection DoS by exploiting the fact that ConnectToDb() has no connection timeout, causing the gorm.Open() call to block indefinitely if the DB host is unreachable, stalling the application startup?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of input validation on dbConfig.Pool.MaxLifetime (set via sqlDb.SetConnMaxLifetime()) to keep connections alive indefinitely if MaxLifetime is 0, preventing connection recycling and accumulating stale or compromised connections?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a misconfigured MaxIdleConnections of 0 (which disables the idle connection pool) to force a new DB connection on every request, causing connection exhaustion and latency amplification under moderate load?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a denial of service by exploiting the fact that ConnectToDb() does not retry on failure, so a transient DB unavailability at startup results in a permanently broken DbClient being returned to callers?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the password field is included in the DSN string passed to postgres.Open(), and if gorm.Open() returns an error, the full DSN (including password) may be embedded in the error message logged by log.Warn(err)?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a goroutine leak or resource leak by triggering repeated failed ConnectToDb() calls, since there is no cleanup of partially initialized gorm.DB objects when db.DB() fails at line 25?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a maximum value cap on dbConfig.Pool.MaxOpenConnections to cause the PostgreSQL server to reject all new connections (max_connections exceeded), effectively denying service to all other clients of the database?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that ConnectToDb() logs 'Successfully connected to database' (line 22) on success, potentially leaking the fact that a DB connection is active and confirming the attack surface to an attacker monitoring log output?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the use of gormlogrus.New() as the GORM logger to cause verbose SQL query logging, which may expose sensitive query parameters (e.g., account IDs, transaction hashes) in log output accessible to the attacker?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the gorm logger (gormlogrus.New()) logs slow queries or errors with full SQL text, leaking internal schema details or query structure that aids in crafting further attacks?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: GetDbWithContext()] Can an unprivileged external user bypass the statement timeout entirely by triggering a code path where statementTimeout <= 0 (line 23), causing GetDbWithContext() to return a DB handle with no timeout, allowing long-running queries that block DB connections indefinitely?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: GetDbWithContext()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a DoS by exploiting the noop cancel function returned when statementTimeout <= 0, since callers that call the returned cancel function expect it to release resources, but noop() does nothing, leaving contexts open?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: GetDbWithContext()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fallback to context.Background() when ctx is nil (line 32) to bypass any deadline or cancellation set by the HTTP request context, allowing queries to outlive their originating HTTP request?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: GetDbWithContext()] Can an unprivileged external user cause resource exhaustion by sending many concurrent API requests that each trigger GetDbWithContext() with statementTimeout > 0, creating many context.WithTimeout child contexts that are never cancelled if callers ignore the returned CancelFunc?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: '] [Function: GetDbWithContext()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that GetDbWithContext() creates a new child context with context.WithTimeout() on every call (line 36), and if the cancel function is not called by the caller, this leaks context resources proportional to request rate?
